So when somebody lands on the landing page and then enters in their email, we call this conversion number, the landing page conversion, LPC, landing page conversion. This other one here was click through rate. And so we see typically 20% or above. If you're above 20%, then you're good. If you're below 20%, then you've got to do a couple of iterations and improvements to really test it and get closer to 20%. And then what happens is someone will enter in their email address, and then they will come to our value video. And so they'll enter in their their email like this, and they come to this video here, this video starts playing, they watch the video, and then they come here to apply. And so the next step in our process here is the VCV. You can see here, it's the people who go from the value video, and then they apply for a strategy session and a call by completing the survey. And what we typically see in between the value video and the survey is we see 5%. So from people who come here and give us our email address and then click here and then schedule a strategy session with us and complete the survey, we only see 5% of those people do that. And so that means if 100 people opt in on our landing page and give us their email address, only five of them will actually go through and schedule a call and complete the survey. So 5%. Now it can be above, but 5% is really the minimum threshold that we need to make this a profitable activity for us. And so again, it's very easy to hit a 5% value video conversion rate if you have a proof of concept established from organic methods. And we typically pay in between $50 and $300 to acquire or to get a survey application. And so we call this CPS, which is cost per survey. So we typically, what I see from all the different students who are doing well, they're in between this range here, between $50 and $300. And then the next step is the call. So what we call this here is the show up rate, the SUR. That's the abbreviation for show up rate. And we typically see an 80% show up rate. And what that means is that out of, say, out of, say, 100 people that complete the survey form and schedule a call in our calendar, we will only really have a call with 80% of those people. And what that means is 20% of them we're not having a call with. Now typically, how that number works, well, that means that 20% of the people who complete that survey, we're not talking to. So where do those 20% of people go? Well, there's two places people can go and there's two things that determine a show up rate. And number one is canceled applications. So that means we're looking through people's surveys and we reject them because they're not a good fit. So we ourselves look through, we're like, hmm, I don't think this person's right for my business. So we send them an email, we cancel it, we delete it out of our calendar. So that's one thing that can make up a show up rate. The other thing is that we can have some no shows. So those are people that complete the survey, we accept the survey and we're like, yep, this person looks good, we're going to talk to them. And then we go to call them at the time and they don't pick up their phone or nothing happens. It just rings through the voicemail and it's just a no show. We call that a no show. And so typically what I see is people cancel about 10% of appointments and then their no show rate is about 10% of appointments. And so that means that together 20% of applications don't end up becoming a call. So our show up rate is typically 80%. That's what we see across the board with our successful students in this consulting program. And that means that our cost per call or cost per strategy session is in between $50 and $300 or it could be a little bit more than that if we're adjusting for our show up rate here. And then once we get on the call with a person and we take them through our conversion script, well then they'll either become a customer or they won't and we call this the call conversion rate CCR. And so like I said we typically convert one in five people when we have our proof of concept established. That means that if we talk to five people one of them will sign up or if we talk to 100 people 20 of them will sign up. And that means that we have a 20% call conversion rate. And what that means is that we pay on average $250 to $1,500 and this depends on different niches, different countries, different varying levels of skill. I've provided ranges for all of these numbers here because you know everyone is a little bit, everyone's circumstances are a little bit different. But across the board we typically see these numbers fall within these ranges here. And so we pay in between $250 to $1,500 to acquire a customer. And we call that a CPA and that's a cost per acquisition. So the cost to acquire a customer. And you're probably thinking well that's a lot of numbers and what do they all mean? Which means that they're, you know, they're getting a 100 to 200% return on their investment. Which means that if they're spending $100 on ads, then they're getting back in between $200 and $300. So I've called, we call that a two to one return or a three to one return as a ratio. Because if we put a dollar in the machine, then we have $2 come out. That's a 100% ROI. Or if we put a dollar in a machine, and $3 comes out, we call that a 200% ROI and a three to one return on investment. And the reason why our numbers are a little bit different to typical investments like where you put your money in a bank and you get like 1% interest or 4% interest or whatever. The reason why ours are a lot higher than that is because we're getting back our money as well as the interest earned on top of it. So if we put a dollar in a machine and we get $2 out, the reason why we call that a 100% return on investment is because we're getting an additional dollar back as well as our original dollar back. And then each time we want to earn more interest, we have to put the dollar back in the machine. However, with a bank account, you've never spent your money, it's still, it's always sitting in that account. So do you remember how we discussed natural selection earlier this week and the alchemy of client attraction module? So we really talked about, you know, lions and nature and everything and and different beetles and nature and how like different beetles, we could have like a brown colored beetle and we could have a green colored beetle. And there could be a bird that prefers to eat green colored beetles or over the brown colored beetles. And so naturally, you know, the beetles want to try and evolve so that they can become the different color and escape the bird. Well, that's what we call natural selection or survival of the fittest. And when it comes to lions in nature, you know, when a lion is hungry and it's looking for, you know, it's praying, it's looking for something to eat, it might be stalking a herd of zebra and there might be, you know, a thousand zebra or something there. And it's looking at them and it can't chase all of the zebra at the same time. It's impossible. So it's trying to think, which one am I going to go for? Because if I go for them, I need to just go for one. Otherwise, I'm not going to catch anything. And so the lion uses natural selection. It walks out in front of the zebra, it kind of scares them and the zebra start running. And then typically what happens is a few of the zebra at the back, they're either younger or they're wounded or whatever, they will kind of hang back at the back of the pack at the back of the herd as it starts running away. And then once the zebra can kind of identify that small herd, it'll start running for them. Once the lion can, it'll start running for them. And then as it's chasing after them, that small herd will start to fragment to and one zebra will fall back behind. And then that lion will go for that one zebra. And that's how the lion thins the herd from, you know, 500 zebra all the way down to one zebra. And that is what we call natural selection. And we have that similar problem to the lion when it comes to client attraction. And we discussed this in the Alchemy of Client Attraction module earlier in this week. So the big problem with attraction for us is that only 3% of any given market is ready to buy right now. And we need to know who these 3% are. So if there's 100 people on the screen and the ones with the green tick next to them are the ones that are going to buy, well, we need to know who these three are. And how do we know who these 3% are? Well, we use natural selection just like the lion does. And our paid client attraction method is natural selection on steroids. And let me show you how it is. Let me show you how this paid client attraction method, which I've just shown you, is just pure natural selection. It's natural selection on steroids. So here's our entire process from start to finish. And here's how it filters down. You know, we start off with just our ads. We're showing our ads to people, people are clicking on them.
